Cost Efficiency = API-equivalent cost (last 30d) ÷ subscription cost (per month).
API cost is computed from actual token usage at each model's per-token API price (output tokens only — conservative lower bound). A ratio above 1.0 means the subscription is cheaper than pay-per-use API; below 1.0 means API would save money.

Estimated Value per session = hourly_rate × log2(events) × hours_factor × displacement
hourly_rate: employee paygrade ($/hr), proxy for the cost of their time.
hours_factor: estimated time impact per unit of log2(events) — non-work = 0, trivial = 0.05, useful = 0.25, harmful = −0.30.
displacement = 0.5 on positive contributions only (saved time isn't 1:1 fungible with paid hourly rate — counterfactual uncertainty, context-switch overhead). Negative contributions from harmful sessions are not discounted: cleanup time after a hallucination is real wall-clock time spent recovering.
Example: a useful 64-message session at $100/hr ≈ $75 net positive; a harmful 16-message session at $100/hr ≈ −$120 (1+ hour spent recovering from a wrong-direction debugging spiral).

Harmful sessions: not assigned at ingest time. The cheap classifier only emits useful / trivial / non-work. Periodically, a stronger model and accumulated user feedback (thumbs-down, manual flags) re-review a sample of sessions and override the original verdict — sessions where AI hallucinated, sent the user down the wrong path, or produced confidently-wrong code that had to be reverted get re-tagged as harmful. The QA review timestamp and reason are stored alongside the session.

ROI = estimated value (last 30d) ÷ subscription cost (per month).
Above 1.0 means the company is getting more value than it spends. This is a directional metric — the absolute numbers are proxies, but relative comparisons across users and departments are meaningful.
<strong>Analytics</strong> — where acros: Analytics — where across the org AI accelerates work vs. slows it down, which tasks land and which get abandoned.
<strong>Analytics</strong> — where in th: Analytics — where in the org AI accelerates work vs. slows it down, and which tasks land vs. get abandoned.
<strong>Company knowledge base</strong>: Company knowledge base — a living corpus of problems your people have actually figured out, useful for onboarding and reference.
Company knowledge base — a living corpus of problems your people have actually solved using AI, useful for onboarding and reference.
<strong>Cost &amp; ROI</strong> — which: Cost & ROI — which AI subscriptions pay for themselves, which are dead weight, which cost center to charge.
<strong>Exec / manager in your company</: Exec / manager in your company — aggregates, insights, and the company knowledge base. Never an individual developer's raw session.
Exec / manager in your company — aggregates, insights, and the company knowledge base. Never an individual's raw session.
<strong>Harmful sessions</strong> aren't: Harmful sessions aren't tagged at ingest. A post-hoc QA pass (stronger model + user feedback) re-reviews sessions and re-classifies the ones where AI sent the user down the wrong path. This drags total value down on teams where AI is being misused or trusted too readily.
<strong>Insights</strong> — cross-team s: Insights — cross-team signals (who is suddenly asking security's questions?), paygrade alignment, negative-ROI and prevented-harm patterns.
<strong>Privacy mode OFF</strong> — mana: Privacy mode OFF — managers in the same company can view individual sessions, like any coding-productivity tool.
Privacy mode OFF — managers in the same company can view individual sessions, like any ordinary productivity tool.
<strong>Privacy mode ON</strong> (defaul: Privacy mode ON (default for EU / works-council contexts) — no. Only the developer themselves. Aggregates enforce a minimum cohort size so small teams don't indirectly identify anyone.
Privacy mode ON (default for EU / works-council contexts) — no. Only the employee themselves. Aggregates enforce a minimum cohort size so small teams don't indirectly identify anyone.
<strong>The employee</strong> — their ow: The employee — their own AI sessions in full.
<strong>discount = 0.5 on positive contr: discount = 0.5 on positive contributions only: saved time isn't 1:1 fungible with paid hourly rate (the alternative might have been faster, or not strictly necessary). Harmful sessions skip the discount — cleanup time after a hallucination is real wall-clock time spent recovering.

efficiency = API-equivalent cost (last 3: efficiency = API-equivalent cost (last 30d) ÷ subscription cost (per month)
seerai acts as <strong>processor</strong: seerai acts as processor under GDPR Art. 28; your company is the controller. A DPA signed at onboarding defines the scope.
seerai is an observer layer for the AI-a: seerai is an observer layer for the AI-assistant conversations happening across your organization — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, internal LLM apps, any of it. It turns scattered chat logs into organizational insight (which tools pay off, where AI accelerates work, what your people are actually doing with it) without exposing any one employee's conversations to their manager.
seerai is an observer layer for your dev: seerai is an observer layer for your developers' AI coding sessions. It turns raw chat logs into organizational insight — which tools pay off, where AI accelerates work, what your people are actually solving — without exposing any individual developer's work to their manager.
